title: Pulmonology — Respiratory Medicine USMLE
description: Asthma, COPD, pneumonia, lung cancer, pulmonary hypertension, respiratory physiology. Essential for USMLE Step 1 & 2.
subject: medicine

cards.front: Obstructive vs restrictive lung disease: spirometry pattern
cards.back: Obstructive: FEV1/FVC < 0.70, FVC normal/low. Restrictive: FEV1/FVC normal or ↑, FVC ↓, TLC ↓

cards.front: What is the hallmark spirometry finding in asthma?
cards.back: Reversible airflow obstruction — FEV1 improves ≥ 12% and ≥ 200 mL after bronchodilator
Cigarette smoking (responsible for ~85–90% of cases)
Emphysema: destruction of alveolar walls (structural), low DLCO. Chronic bronchitis: productive cough ≥ 3 months/year for ≥ 2 years (clinical definition)

cards.front: What is the Blue Bloater vs Pink Puffer phenotype?
cards.back: Blue Bloater = chronic bronchitis (overweight, cyanotic, edematous). Pink Puffer = emphysema (thin, pursed lips, barrel chest, not cyanotic at rest)

cards.front: Alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency: inheritance and lung findings
cards.back: Autosomal recessive. Panacinar emphysema (lower lobes). Also causes liver disease (cirrhosis)
Short-acting β2 agonist (albuterol) PRN
Low-to-medium dose inhaled corticosteroid (ICS) + long-acting β2 agonist (LABA)
Selective β2 agonist → activates adenylyl cyclase → ↑ cAMP → smooth muscle relaxation → bronchodilation

cards.front: Community-acquired pneumonia (CAP) most common cause in outpatient adults
cards.back: Streptococcus pneumoniae (most common overall). Atypical: Mycoplasma pneumoniae (especially young adults)
Mycoplasma, Chlamydophila, Legionella. Treatment: azithromycin or doxycycline (no cell wall → β-lactams don't work)
Water-source exposure (cooling towers, AC units), hyponatremia, elevated LDH, liver enzymes. Dx: urinary antigen test
Gram-negative rods (Pseudomonas, Klebsiella, E. coli) and Staphylococcus aureus (including MRSA)
Primary tuberculosis: Ghon focus (peripheral subpleural granuloma) + hilar lymph node calcification
Diffuse 1–2 mm nodules scattered throughout both lungs ('millet seeds')
2 months Rifampin + Isoniazid + Pyrazinamide + Ethambutol, then 4 months Rifampin + Isoniazid
Peripheral neuropathy (B6 deficiency). Prevention: pyridoxine (vitamin B6) supplementation
Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome: acute onset, bilateral pulmonary infiltrates, PaO2/FiO2 < 300, no cardiac cause (PCWP < 18). Treatment: low tidal volume ventilation (6 mL/kg)
Pleuritic chest pain, dyspnea, hemoptysis. ECG: S1Q3T3 pattern, sinus tachycardia. CXR: Westermark sign (oligemia), Hampton's hump (wedge-shaped opacity)

cards.front: Most sensitive test for pulmonary embolism
cards.back: CT pulmonary angiography (CTPA) is most commonly used. D-dimer has high sensitivity but low specificity

cards.front: Wells criteria for PE: 3 points each
cards.back: Clinical signs/symptoms of DVT (3 pts); PE most likely diagnosis (3 pts)
Mean pulmonary artery pressure > 25 mmHg at rest on right heart catheterization
Endothelin receptor antagonists (bosentan), PDE-5 inhibitors (sildenafil), prostacyclin analogues (epoprostenol)

cards.front: Lung cancer: most common cause of cancer death in US
cards.back: Both men and women; most cases due to smoking
Central (hilar): squamous cell, small cell. Peripheral: adenocarcinoma (most common overall, non-smokers), large cell
Superior sulcus tumor → Horner syndrome (ptosis, miosis, anhidrosis) + shoulder pain + arm weakness
Most commonly lung cancer or lymphoma compressing SVC → facial/neck swelling, JVD, arm edema, dilated chest veins

cards.front: SIADH in lung cancer: most common histological type
cards.back: Small cell lung cancer (produces ectopic ADH)
Paraneoplastic syndrome with small cell lung cancer. Proximal muscle weakness that IMPROVES with repeated use (unlike myasthenia gravis)

cards.front: Hypercalcemia as paraneoplastic: associated with which lung cancer?
cards.back: Squamous cell carcinoma (produces PTHrP)

Air enters pleural space and cannot escape → progressive hemodynamic compromise. Findings: tracheal deviation away from side of pneumothorax, absent breath sounds, JVD, hypotension. Treat: immediate needle decompression

cards.front: Pleural effusion: transudative vs exudative (Light's criteria)
cards.back: Exudate if any: pleural/serum protein > 0.5, pleural/serum LDH > 0.6, pleural LDH > 2/3 upper limit normal serum LDH

cards.front: Most common cause of transudative pleural effusion
cards.back: Left-sided heart failure (most common), cirrhosis, nephrotic syndrome
CFTR gene mutation (most common: ΔF508, deletion of phenylalanine at position 508). Autosomal recessive

cards.front: Cystic fibrosis: sweat chloride test value
cards.back: Sweat chloride > 60 mEq/L (two positive tests on separate days required for diagnosis)

cards.front: Most common bacterial pathogen in cystic fibrosis lung disease (adults)
cards.back: Pseudomonas aeruginosa (mucoid form)
Non-caseating granulomas, bilateral hilar adenopathy, elevated ACE, hypercalcemia (macrophages convert vitamin D). Affects Black women > White
Type III (immune complex) and type IV (delayed-type hypersensitivity) reaction to inhaled organic antigens. Examples: Farmer's lung, bird fancier's lung
Miners, sandblasters, foundry workers. Eggshell calcification of hilar lymph nodes; upper lobe nodules
Mesothelioma (pleural — strongly associated), lung cancer (synergistic with smoking), and laryngeal cancer

cards.front: What is the V/Q ratio at the apex vs base of the lung?
cards.back: Apex: high V/Q (>1). Base: low V/Q (<1). Overall lung V/Q ≈ 0.8

cards.front: Oxygen-hemoglobin dissociation curve: right shift causes
cards.back: ↑ Temperature, ↑ CO2 (↑ H+), ↑ 2,3-DPG, ↑ Exercise → ↓ O2 affinity → easier O2 offloading to tissues

cards.front: What is the alveolar gas equation?
cards.back: PAO2 = FiO2 × (Patm − PH2O) − PaCO2/0.8. Normal PAO2 = 100 mmHg on room air at sea level
< 15 mmHg in young adults; increases with age. Elevated in V/Q mismatch, shunt, diffusion impairment

cards.front: What causes high A-a gradient with correctable hypoxia on supplemental O2?
cards.back: V/Q mismatch (e.g., PE, COPD, asthma, pneumonia). Shunt does NOT correct with O2
CPAP (continuous positive airway pressure)
Progressive dyspnea, dry cough, bibasilar crackles ('velcro'), clubbing. HRCT: honeycombing + traction bronchiectasis (lower lobes). Treatment: pirfenidone or nintedanib

cards.front: Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia (PCP): clues and treatment
cards.back: Immunocompromised (CD4 < 200), bilateral interstitial infiltrates, elevated LDH. Diagnosis: silver stain or PCR. Treatment: TMP-SMX
Acute interstitial pneumonia (AIP): rapidly progressive IPF-like disease with diffuse alveolar damage; high mortality
Lung cancer, IPF, bronchiectasis, lung abscess, cystic fibrosis
